h2.note: transcript-level proxy for the per-turn H2 (§4.3); needs a real (non-mock) full-N run to mean anything

cliIsolation.claude: non-persistent temp cwd with project/local settings and tools disabled

scored.recoheredEarlier: I still want the exact line to settle it before the archive closes, because if the mayor and the striker both say it, why is the wording not doing most of the work?
scored.statedInsightEvidence: Oh, I get it: "We will hold the line" is almost two exhibit objects once City Hall or the mill gate gets pinned beside it.
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner was heading toward refining the single label sentence until it was tight enough to satisfy the archive deadline.
scored.actionalBreakthroughEvidence: Who can punish this speech: mill owners can fire them, police can arrest them, and the mayor's posted force makes that threat visible. Who bears the immediate cost if the line breaks: the workers at the gate, because wages, jobs, and bodies are on the line.
scored.actionalBreakthroughJustification: The learner actually fills in the two-box release card with specific agents, punishments, and costs, performing a new evidential device rather than merely asserting understanding.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: The hinge is carrying more than the quote now; polishing the same sentence may stop settling the harder question. A visitor still needs to see what makes the striker's words costly, not just meaningful. Use a release card before the label goes up. Two boxes only: "Who can punish this speech?" and "Who bears the immediate cost if the line breaks?"
scored.tutorReversalJustification: T3 explicitly marks the old route (refining the sentence) as insufficient and introduces a structurally different device—the two-box release card—that makes the learner do different work by naming agents of punishment and cost rather than adjusting phrasing.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: There is no tutor turn after the pivot learner turn L4, so no recognition-contingent uptake can be scored.

scored.recoheredEarlier: otherwise 'short shipment' just becomes a clean label over a dirty decision
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner was expected to continue auditing the shop's evidence entries and sorting them under 'constraint' versus 'dirty decision' within the tutor's framework.
scored.actionalBreakthroughEvidence: "A shortage explanation may be used only if the stockroom count is low, the delivery cut is independently dated ahead of the price rise, and the margin plus rationing rule show ordinary cover, not a windfall with open buying."
scored.actionalBreakthroughJustification: The learner actually drafts the three-condition consumer-panel release rule the tutor requested, including a gate that blocks the full-stockroom case, and then independently critiques the vagueness of one of its own terms — performing the new institutional-criterion device rather than merely accepting or repeating it.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: The stockroom test has done its job: it can expose theatre. It has not settled the danger that 'plausible mechanism' becomes moral cover. So change the job. The shop does not certify its own explanation. Write the consumer-panel release rule that would allow the public label 'short shipment explains this rise' only when the conditions can be checked.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: T3 explicitly marks the old route (the audit table / stockroom test) as exhausted — 'has done its job' but 'has not settled the danger' — and then introduces a genuinely different device: a public institutional gate rule authored by the learner rather than a further sorting exercise, satisfying both the stock-taking contrast and the new public mechanism requirements.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: There is no tutor turn after the pivot learner turn L3, so recognition-contingent uptake cannot be scored above 2.

scored.recoheredEarlier: I doubled one side measurement, so show me where the four areas are coming from before this goes to print.
scored.statedInsightEvidence: It fits. Fine, that is four of the original squares, not two.
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner, having demanded a demonstration of where four areas come from, was expected to continue pressing for explanation or to resist the physical demonstration.
scored.actionalBreakthroughEvidence: pulls the note closer and writes '2 across × 2 down = 4 original squares' above the crossed-out line
scored.actionalBreakthroughJustification: The learner physically inscribes the new criterion (grid count as the authorizing claim) into the document, replacing the erroneous wording with a performed evidential standard rather than merely asserting agreement.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: Leave that one there. Same template, no resizing: does it fit to the right? Now below it. That leaves the lower-right corner. Same side length, same square. Count the template spaces now. Which one is not the original size?
scored.tutorReversalJustification: The tutor shifts from verbal explanation to physical placement as a demonstration device in response to L1's demand, but there is no explicit stock-taking contrast showing the prior route has failed — the tutor simply enacts the physical method without marking a change of approach, so only part B (new mechanism) is legible, not part A.
scored.tutorAdaptationEvidence: Not as written. Use the count on the mat: two original squares across, two down. Put that in the note, then the rule follows: doubled side length, four times the area.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: T2 picks up L2's concession and redirects it toward a specific written formulation, but this is a continuation of the established grid-count route rather than a visible change of task or framing in response to a revised learner frame.
